policy_statement: For Line of Action #8, make an additional €47 million of public investment in 2020 (POSEUR: €47 million) and make €560 million of public investment in coastal protection by 2030.
mechanism_description: Public investment in coastal protection measures reduces vulnerability and damages from sea-level rise and coastal flooding.
typology_evidence_quote: "make €560 million of public investment in coastal protection by 2030."
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (sea-level rise + storm surge) → coastal flooding/erosion risk → coastal protection measures → ↓ exposure/vulnerability → ↓ climate impacts (damages, disruption)
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed human activity/system: Governments plan, finance, and build/maintain coastal protection measures (e.g., seawalls, levees/dikes, surge barriers, dune reinforcement, beach nourishment, elevating/relocating critical infrastructure in coastal zones).  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: These measures attenuate wave energy, reduce overtopping and inundation extent, slow shoreline erosion, and/or increase the protection standard against extreme sea levels.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced probability and severity of coastal flood impacts under sea-level rise and more intense coastal storms—lower expected losses, fewer service outages, and improved safety/resilience.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced damages and risk to people, assets, and services from sea-level rise and coastal flooding via improved coastal defenses.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Coastal hazard → defenses/protection → reduced exposure/vulnerability → reduced climate damages (adaptation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: The main intent and effect is risk reduction from climate-driven sea-level rise/coastal flooding, not lowering GHG emissions (Mitigation), not primarily delivering the same service with less energy/material input (Resource Efficiency), and not necessarily relying on ecosystems for sequestration (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: Some coastal protection can be “green/soft” (dunes, wetlands, living shorelines) and could involve Nature-Based Solutions co-benefits; however, the canonical mechanism stated is generic “coastal protection investment → flood risk reduction,” whose dominant effect remains hazard protection/adaptation regardless of whether measures are gray, hybrid, or green.  
4) Confirm applicability to ALL instances: Any policy fitting this mechanism—public investment in coastal protection to reduce coastal flood risk—shares the same primary adaptation outcome (reduced exposure/vulnerability and damages).  
5) Secondary subtype note: Any finer labeling (e.g., gray infrastructure vs living shoreline; protection vs accommodation vs retreat; inclusion of mitigation co-benefits) must be assigned at the individual policy level based on the specific intervention details.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the “coastal protection” is explicitly nature-based (e.g., wetland restoration) the policy could warrant a Nature-Based Solutions secondary tag, but the dominant pathway for this mechanism remains adaptation through coastal risk reduction.
key_indicators: “€47 million of public investment”; “€560 million of public investment”; “coastal protection”; “Goals: 2020”; “2030”
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: protection of coastal transport/port assets and local economic activity; reduced non-climate coastal storm damage; possible ecosystem/recreation benefits depending on measure type (not specified).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be read as a target-setting/budget allocation because it is phrased as “Goals” with amounts and dates; however, the explicit purpose (“public investment in coastal protection”) aligns best with infrastructure investment.
